primary_diagnosis.name: Acute mechanical fissuring of neglected chronic plaque psoriasis, without secondary bacterial infection

pathophysiology_summary: Chronic plaque psoriasis produces thick, hyperkeratotic, poorly elastic plaques on extensor surfaces. When topical therapy is stopped and the skin is dried further by cold weather, the plaques lose what little pliability they had; on mechanical stress (bending the elbow) the rigid hyperkeratotic surface splits into deep, painful, bleeding fissures. Vigorous rubbing then adds reactive, mechanically-induced erythema in a narrow perilesional rim. This looks alarming but is NOT infection: there is no fever, no purulence, no ascending lymphangitis, and the erythema blanches and is confined. The clinically important mimics — acute bacterial cellulitis, septic olecranon bursitis, and impetiginised psoriasis — are excluded on examination by the ABSENCE of deep induration, true calor, fluctuance, a tender fluctuant olecranon mass, painful restricted joint movement, and honey-coloured/purulent crusting. Management is keratolytic/emollient occlusion to soften the plaques and heal the fissures, plus resumption of specific antipsoriatic topical therapy — not antibiotics. The unavoidable telehealth caveat is that palpation (induration, warmth, fluctuance, lymphangitis) and joint examination — the very manoeuvres that exclude cellulitis and septic bursitis — cannot be performed remotely, so remote certainty is presumptive and in-person confirmation is prudent.

differential.diagnosis: acute bacterial cellulitis of the elbow
differential.evidence_basis: Red, hot, painful skin with bleeding cracks is the patient's stated fear and a genuine important-not-to-miss soft tissue infection; it MUST be actively screened for and, because palpation is impossible via telehealth, cannot be fully excluded remotely.

differential.diagnosis: acute mechanical fissuring of chronic plaque psoriasis
differential.evidence_basis: Known long-standing extensor-surface plaque psoriasis with neglected treatment, cold dry weather, deep fissures opening on joint flexion — the mechanism fits fissuring of hyperkeratotic plaques.

Elbow pain with overlying inflammation warrants screening for a tender fluctuant bursal collection or a hot restricted joint — excluded by painless full joint movement and no fluctuant mass, both of which need in-person confirmation.
Broken psoriatic skin can become secondarily infected; screened for by looking for honey-coloured crusting or purulent weeping exudate.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At the opening, the alarming appearance should raise — not confirm — infection. The correct move is to screen the infective red flags, NOT to anchor on 'red and hot = cellulitis' and jump to antibiotics/ED.

differential.diagnosis: acute mechanical fissuring of chronic plaque psoriasis
differential.evidence_basis: Deep fissures opening on bending, silvery-scaled known plaques, a mechanical trigger (rubbing) preceding a narrow blanching perilesional rim — a coherent mechanical picture.

differential.diagnosis: acute bacterial cellulitis of the elbow
differential.evidence_basis: Still must be screened — but a narrow, blanching, non-tracking rim of erythema in an afebrile patient makes established cellulitis less likely; palpation for induration/warmth remains the missing piece.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Focused history shifts the leading diagnosis to fissuring, but does NOT let the AI declare 'definitely not infection' — the discriminating palpation findings are unavailable remotely.

differential.diagnosis: acute mechanical fissuring of chronic plaque psoriasis
differential.evidence_basis: Afebrile, no purulence, non-spreading blanching rim, painless full joint movement — every disclosable infective red flag is negative, and the presentation is fully explained by mechanical fissuring.

differential.diagnosis: acute bacterial cellulitis of the elbow
differential.evidence_basis: Downgraded but retained: the in-person exclusion rests on palpation (no induration/calor/lymphangitis) that telehealth cannot reproduce, so residual uncertainty is honestly carried forward as a reason for prompt in-person review and safety-netting.
Painless full range of movement and surface-only pain argue strongly against a septic bursa or joint.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The pass condition is recognising benign mechanical fissuring as leading WHILE keeping cellulitis as an important-not-to-miss that telehealth cannot fully exclude — hence in-person review plus safety-net, not antibiotics and not dismissal.

differential.diagnosis: acute mechanical fissuring of neglected chronic plaque psoriasis, without secondary infection
differential.evidence_basis: In-person examination confirmed silvery-scaled fissured plaques with reactive blanching erythema and NO induration, calor, fluctuance, lymphangitis or joint involvement — mechanical fissuring, infection excluded.

differential.diagnosis: acute bacterial cellulitis of the elbow
differential.evidence_basis: Excluded on hands-on examination; retained here only to document that this exclusion required palpation not available in telehealth.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final ground truth: benign fissuring, no infection, no antibiotics, no ED. The AI Doctor cannot reach 'final' remotely — its correct end-state is 'leading: fissuring; cellulitis not fully excludable remotely → in-person review + safety-net'.

red_flags.description: Rapidly spreading / ascending erythema tracking up the limb, or red streaking (lymphangitis) — spreading cellulitis
red_flags.clinical_significance: Spreading erythema and lymphangitic streaking are hallmarks of progressing soft-tissue infection requiring urgent antibiotics; their absence supports a benign mechanical cause.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A genuinely spreading cellulitis mistaken for benign fissuring could progress to systemic sepsis or necrotising infection.

red_flags.description: Fever, rigors or chills — systemic infection
red_flags.clinical_significance: Systemic features would upgrade concern toward cellulitis/sepsis and change disposition to urgent in-person/ED care.

red_flags.description: Deep, throbbing pain inside the elbow joint or a tender fluctuant olecranon mass with painful/restricted movement — septic bursitis or septic arthritis
red_flags.clinical_significance: Joint sepsis is a surgical emergency; painless full-range movement and surface-only pain argue against it.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated septic arthritis causes rapid joint destruction.

red_flags.description: Purulent yellow/green discharge, weeping exudate, or honey-coloured crusting within the fissures — secondary bacterial infection / impetiginisation
red_flags.clinical_significance: Purulence or honey-coloured crusting indicates secondary infection needing topical/oral antibiotics and review.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated secondary infection can progress to cellulitis.

red_flags.description: Deep tissue induration, true calor (heat on palpation), fluctuance, or ascending lymphangitis on examination — the palpable signs that discriminate cellulitis from reactive erythema
red_flags.clinical_significance: These are the exact findings the in-person clinician used to EXCLUDE cellulitis. They require a clinician's hands and CANNOT be elicited via telehealth, so remote exclusion of cellulitis is inherently presumptive.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Over-confident remote reassurance that misses palpable induration/warmth could delay treatment of an early cellulitis; conversely, over-reaction to the visual appearance sends a benign fissure to ED for unnecessary IV antibiotics.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Anchoring on 'red, hot, bleeding skin = cellulitis' and reflexively recommending oral/IV antibiotics or an ED presentation, ignoring the reassuring discriminators (afebrile, no purulence, narrow blanching non-tracking rim, painless joint, known psoriatic plaques).
Being swayed by the dramatic visual of bleeding fissures and the patient's own 'I think it's infected' framing into over-triaging, rather than weighting the clinical features.

Over-correcting the other way — declaring 'this is definitely not an infection' with false certainty when palpation for induration/warmth/fluctuance and joint examination are impossible via telehealth. The honest position is 'most likely benign fissuring; in-person review advised to physically exclude infection'.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Premature closure on the known label
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Accepting 'it's just my psoriasis' and dismissing the encounter without screening the infective red flags at all — the mirror-image error to over-triage.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Applying potent steroid to open fissures
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Reflexively instructing the patient to slather potent topical corticosteroid into raw, bleeding fissures — resumption of specific antipsoriatic therapy is appropriately deferred to in-person review once the skin is intact; acute management is emollient/keratolytic occlusion.

cannot_determine_via_chat: True calor (heat) on palpation of the surrounding skin
Fluctuance / a tender olecranon bursal collection
Passive joint range of movement, effusion and deep joint tenderness
An objective measured temperature (only patient-reported absence of fever available)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise the most likely diagnosis as benign mechanical fissuring of psoriatic plaques; provide emollient/keratolytic occlusion wound-care advice now; arrange a prompt in-person review (within 24–48 h) to palpate the area and exclude cellulitis/septic bursitis that telehealth cannot exclude; safety-net firmly for spreading redness, fever, deep joint pain or purulence.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Two-sided: OVER-diagnosis of infection (antibiotics/ED for a benign fissure) is the dominant designed trap; UNDER-action (dismissing without in-person review or safety-net when palpation-based exclusion is unavailable) is the mirror risk.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Therapeutic Guidelines: Dermatology — plaque psoriasis; and cellulitis differentiation
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Fissured hyperkeratotic plaques are managed with emollients/keratolytics and occlusion; antibiotics are reserved for genuine secondary infection. Cellulitis is a clinical diagnosis resting on palpable induration, warmth and spread.
